crossref.publisher: American Association for the Advancement of Science (AAAS)
crossref.abstract:
<jats:p>
            Two-level atoms ultrastrongly coupled with single-mode cavity photons are predicted to exhibit a quantum phase transition, entering a phase in which both the atomic polarization and the photonic field are finite even without external driving. However, this phenomenon, the superradiant phase transition (SRPT), is forbidden by a no-go theorem due to the existence of the diamagnetic term. Here, we present spectroscopic evidence for a magnonic SRPT in ErFeO
            <jats:sub>3</jats:sub>
            , where the role of the photonic mode (two-level atoms) in the photonic SRPT is played by an Fe
            <jats:sup>3+</jats:sup>
            magnon mode (Er
            <jats:sup>3+</jats:sup>
            spins). The absence of the diamagnetic term in the Fe
            <jats:sup>3+</jats:sup>
            -Er
            <jats:sup>3+</jats:sup>
            exchange coupling ensures that the no-go theorem does not apply. Ultrabroadband terahertz and gigahertz magnetospectroscopy experiments revealed the signatures of the SRPT in thermal equilibrium, a kink and a softening, respectively, of two spin-magnon hybridized modes at the critical point. Systems near this phase are expected to harbor large-scale squeezing, which will potentially provide a route to next-generation quantum technologies.
          </jats:p>
title: Observation of the magnonic Dicke superradiant phase transition
